tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary treatment
tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is a standard chemoradiation option for localized anal canal cancer, supported by the RTOG 98-11 trial, which showed improved 5-year disease-free survival (67.8% vs 57.8%, P=0.006) and overall survival (78.3% vs 70.7%, P=0.026) compared to cisplatin-based chemoRT. Mitomycin is given on Days 1 and 29, capped at 20 mg, with continuous infusion fluorouracil during weeks 1 and 5 of radiation. This combination is considered preferred due to superior efficacy and evidence from randomized trials. Treatment should be administered with intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to reduce toxicity. [1,2]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary treatment
tier_1_regimens.notes: This variant uses a single bolus of mitomycin at 12 mg/m2 on Day 1, capped at 20 mg, with continuous infusion fluorouracil during weeks 1 and 5 of radiation. It is also supported by the RTOG 98-11 trial data. The single-dose mitomycin may be used to reduce toxicity while maintaining efficacy. This regimen is appropriate for patients who may tolerate mitomycin well. The choice between 10 mg/m2 on two days or 12 mg/m2 on one day may depend on institutional protocols. Both are considered preferred options. [1,2]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary treatment

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily Monday-Friday, on days of radiation treatment only, throughout the duration of RT (typically 28-30 treatment days)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine is an oral fluoropyrimidine prodrug used as an alternative to intravenous 5-fluorouracil in chemoradiation for anal cancer. It is given twice daily on radiation treatment days, providing continuous radiation sensitization. This regimen is supported by retrospective studies and phase II trials showing similar efficacy and potentially lower hematologic toxicity compared to 5-FU. Mitomycin is given on Days 1 and 29, capped at 20 mg. This option is convenient for patients and is considered preferred. DPYD testing should be considered prior to initiation due to fluoropyrimidine-associated toxicity risks. [3,4]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary treatment

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily Monday-Friday, on days of radiation treatment only, throughout the duration of RT (typically 28-30 treatment days)

tier_1_regimens.notes: This variant uses a single bolus of mitomycin at 12 mg/m2 on Day 1 with capecitabine given twice daily on radiation days. It is an alternative to the two-dose mitomycin schedule, based on similar principles as the 5-FU regimen. The single-dose mitomycin may reduce treatment complexity. This regimen is also preferred for localized anal canal cancer. Capecitabine dosing is the same as above, and DPYD testing is recommended. [3,4]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary treatment
tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen replaces mitomycin with cisplatin in chemoradiation for anal cancer. It is considered an alternative but has category 2B designation due to inferior outcomes in RTOG 98-11, where 5-year DFS was 57.8% vs 67.8% for mitomycin (P=0.006). However, it may be used in patients who cannot tolerate mitomycin. The regimen is administered every 4 weeks with radiation. Cisplatin can cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity, so hydration and electrolyte monitoring are required. [5]

tier_1_regimens.site: Perianal cancer, T1,N0 well or moderately differentiated or select T2,N0 (that does not involve sphincter)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary treatment
tier_1_regimens.notes: Local excision with 1-cm margins is an option for select perianal cancers that are superficial, well or moderately differentiated, and do not involve the sphincter. After excision, if margins are adequate, observation is recommended. If margins are inadequate, re-excision is preferred, or local RT with or without chemotherapy may be considered. This approach aims to preserve sphincter function and avoid the morbidity of chemoradiation. Careful surveillance is necessary due to risk of recurrence. [ANAL-2]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Re-excision or Local RT ± Fluorouracil/Mitomycin or Capecitabine/Mitomycin or Cisplatin/Fluorouracil
tier_1_regimens.site: Perianal cancer with inadequate margins after local excision
tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary treatment for inadequate margins
tier_1_regimens.notes: When local excision of perianal cancer results in inadequate margins, re-excision is the preferred option. If re-excision is not feasible, local radiation therapy with or without concurrent chemotherapy (fluorouracil/mitomycin, capecitabine/mitomycin, or cisplatin/fluorouracil category 2B) can be considered. The choice depends on tumor characteristics and patient factors. The goal is to achieve local control while minimizing toxicity. [ANAL-2]

tier_1_regimens.site: Metastatic anal cancer (or inguinal node recurrence)
tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line therapy for metastatic disease or recurrent inguinal node disease

drugs.name: Retifanlimab-dlwr
drugs.schedule: Day 1, repeat every 28 days for 6 cycles, then followed by Retifanlimab-dlwr 500 mg IV Day 1 repeat every 28 days for up to 7 cycles

trial_data.os: 29.2 months for retifanlimab arm vs 23.0 months for chemotherapy arm
trial_data.pfs: 9.30 months for retifanlimab arm vs 7.39 months for chemotherapy arm

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen combines chemotherapy with the PD-1 inhibitor retifanlimab for first-line treatment of metastatic or recurrent anal cancer. It is based on the POD1UM-303/InterAACT 2 trial, which met its primary endpoint with improved PFS (HR=0.63, P=0.0006) and a trend toward improved OS. Retifanlimab is given concurrently with carboplatin/paclitaxel for 6 cycles, followed by maintenance retifanlimab for up to 7 cycles. This is the preferred first-line option. Alternate checkpoint inhibitor therapy options include cemiplimab-rwlc, dostarlimab-gxly, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, penpulimab-kcqx, tislelizumab-jsgr, or toripalimab-tpzi due to drug class effect. [13,21]

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line or second-line therapy (if not previously given)
tier_1_regimens.notes: CAPEOX is a platinum-based chemotherapy regimen used for metastatic anal cancer. It is an alternative to carboplatin/paclitaxel and is listed as other recommended in first-line and second-line settings. The regimen is repeated every 3 weeks. Oxaliplatin should be discontinued after 3 to 4 months or sooner for neurotoxicity, while maintaining capecitabine until progression. DPYD testing is recommended before capecitabine initiation. This regimen is based on data from colorectal cancer and extrapolated to anal cancer. [6]

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line or second-line therapy (if not previously given)
tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen uses carboplatin AUC 5 with paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 every 21 days. It is an alternative to the retifanlimab combination and is based on the InterAACT trial, which showed similar response rates but better tolerability compared to cisplatin/fluorouracil. Median OS was 20 months and PFS was 8.1 months. Another dosing schedule uses paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 weekly. This is an other recommended option for first-line therapy and second-line if not previously given. [7,8]

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line or second-line therapy (if not previously given)

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 bolus then 1000 mg/m2/day x 2 days
drugs.schedule: bolus Day 1, then continuous infusion over 46-48 hours

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFCIS is a combination of leucovorin, fluorouracil, and cisplatin given every 2 weeks. It is an other recommended option for metastatic anal cancer. A retrospective study of 53 patients showed an ORR of 48%, PFS of 7.1 months, and OS of 22.1 months. Cisplatin and leucovorin are given concurrently on Day 1, followed by fluorouracil bolus and infusion. This regimen is used based on extrapolation and retrospective data. [9]

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line or second-line therapy (if not previously given)

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 bolus then 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days
drugs.schedule: bolus Day 1, then continuous infusion over 46-48 hours

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFOX is a standard chemotherapy regimen used in gastrointestinal cancers and is listed as an other recommended option for metastatic anal cancer. It is given every 2 weeks. The panel recommends considering discontinuation of oxaliplatin after 3 to 4 months due to neurotoxicity while maintaining fluorouracil and leucovorin until progression. Oxaliplatin may be reintroduced if discontinued for neurotoxicity rather than disease progression. Safety data in anal cancer are limited, but it is used based on consensus. [10]

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line or second-line therapy (if not previously given)
tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen uses cisplatin 60 mg/m2 with continuous infusion fluorouracil 1000 mg/m2/day for 4 days, repeated every 3 weeks. It has category 2B designation due to higher toxicity compared to carboplatin/paclitaxel in the InterAACT trial, with more grade ≥3 toxicity (76% vs 71%) and serious adverse events (62% vs 36%). Median OS was 12.3 months and PFS was 5.7 months. It is an other recommended option for patients who have not received platinum-based therapy previously. [11]

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line or second-line therapy (if not previously given)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Modified DCF is a triplet chemotherapy regimen given every 2 weeks. It is an other recommended option for metastatic anal cancer, with category 2B designation due to concerns about higher toxicity. A phase II trial showed a median PFS of 11.0 months for the modified regimen, with 53% of patients experiencing grade 3-4 adverse events. The most common toxicities were neutropenia, diarrhea, and asthenia. This regimen may be considered for patients with good performance status. [14]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent therapy (if no prior immunotherapy received)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cemiplimab-rwlc is a PD-1 inhibitor approved for cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma and extrapolated to anal cancer. It is listed as a preferred option for second-line therapy based on extrapolation from other cancers. The dose is 350 mg IV every 3 weeks. There are no specific biomarker requirements. Toxicities are immune-related and should be managed per NCCN guidelines for immune checkpoint inhibitor toxicities. [15,16]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent therapy (if no prior immunotherapy received)

drugs.name: Dostarlimab-gxly
drugs.schedule: every 3 weeks for 4 doses followed by 1000 mg IV every 6 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dostarlimab-gxly is a PD-1 inhibitor approved for dMMR solid tumors and extrapolated to anal cancer. It is listed as a preferred option for second-line therapy. The dosing involves 500 mg every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then maintenance with 1000 mg every 6 weeks. There is no specific biomarker requirement for anal cancer use. This agent is included based on drug class effect and extrapolation from other indications. [17]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent therapy (if no prior immunotherapy received)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab is a PD-1 inhibitor that has shown activity in metastatic anal cancer in a phase 2 trial (NCI9673), with an ORR of 24% (95% CI, 15-33). The standard dose is 240 mg IV every 2 weeks, but other schedules include 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks, 480 mg every 4 weeks, or 6 mg/kg every 4 weeks. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted. This is a preferred second-line option. [18]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent therapy (if no prior immunotherapy received)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is a PD-1 inhibitor with activity in anal cancer shown in KEYNOTE-028 (ORR 17%) and KEYNOTE-158 (ORR 11% in MSI-H tumors). The dose is 200 mg IV every 3 weeks, with alternatives including 2 mg/kg every 3 weeks, 400 mg every 6 weeks, or 4 mg/kg every 6 weeks. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted. This is a preferred second-line option. [19]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent therapy (if no prior immunotherapy received)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Penpulimab-kcqx is a PD-1 inhibitor added as a preferred option for second-line metastatic anal cancer. The dose is 200 mg IV every 2 weeks. It is included based on extrapolation from other indications and drug class effect. There are no specific biomarker requirements for anal cancer use. This agent is new to the guidelines and provides another immunotherapy option. [20]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent therapy (if no prior immunotherapy received)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Retifanlimab-dlwr is a PD-1 inhibitor studied in the POD1UM-202 trial for second-line metastatic anal cancer, showing an ORR of 13.8% (95% CI, 7.6-22.5%), median PFS of 2.3 months, and OS of 10.1 months. The dose is 500 mg IV every 28 days. This is a preferred second-line option, especially for patients who have not received prior immunotherapy. [21]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent therapy (if no prior immunotherapy received)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Tislelizumab-jsgr is a PD-1 inhibitor listed as a preferred option for second-line metastatic anal cancer. The dose is 200 mg IV every 3 weeks. It is included based on extrapolation from other malignancies and drug class effect. There are no specific biomarker requirements for anal cancer use. This agent provides an additional immunotherapy choice. [22-25]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line and subsequent therapy (if no prior immunotherapy received)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Toripalimab-tpzi is a PD-1 inhibitor listed as a preferred option for second-line metastatic anal cancer. The dose is 3 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks. It is included based on extrapolation from other cancers and drug class effect. There are no specific biomarker requirements for anal cancer use. This agent is part of the expanding immunotherapy options for anal cancer. [26]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Fluorouracil + RT (for local control in metastatic setting)
tier_1_regimens.site: Metastatic anal cancer with symptomatic primary
tier_1_regimens.setting: Chemo/RT for local control

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 24 hours daily on Days 1-5 or 1-7; repeat every 3 weeks for 5 weeks with RT

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen uses continuous infusion fluorouracil at 225 mg/m2/day during radiation for local control in metastatic anal cancer. It is intended for patients with symptomatic bulky primary tumors to achieve palliation. The chemotherapy is given concurrently with radiation, typically for 5 weeks. This is an other recommended option for local control in the metastatic setting. [27-29]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Capecitabine + RT (for local control in metastatic setting)
tier_1_regimens.site: Metastatic anal cancer with symptomatic primary
tier_1_regimens.setting: Chemo/RT for local control

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: twice daily Monday-Friday, on days of radiation treatment only, throughout the duration of RT (typically 28-30 treatment days)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine is used as an oral fluoropyrimidine for chemoradiation in metastatic anal cancer for local control. It is given twice daily on radiation days, providing continuous sensitization. This is an alternative to intravenous fluorouracil and is considered other recommended. It may be used for palliation of symptoms from the primary tumor. DPYD testing is recommended before initiation. [30-32]

tier_2_handbook.definition: Anal carcinoma is a malignancy arising from the anal region, which is divided into the anal canal and the perianal region. The functional anal canal, which is primarily used for radical surgical treatment, is defined by the sphincter muscles. Its superior border is the palpable upper border of the anal sphincter and puborectalis muscles of the anorectal ring, and its inferior border starts at the anal verge, the lowermost edge of the sphincter muscles corresponding to the introitus of the anal orifice. This functional canal is approximately 3 to 5 cm in length. The anatomic anal canal begins at the anorectal ring and extends to the anal verge (squamous mucocutaneous junction with the perianal skin). The perianal region starts at the anal verge and includes the perianal skin over a 5-cm radius from the squamous mucocutaneous junction [ANAL-1, footnote a; ANAL-2, footnote l; MS-6]. Histologically, the proximal anal canal is lined by glandular columnar epithelium, the middle zone by transitional epithelium, and the distal third by nonkeratinizing squamous epithelium, which merges with keratinized perianal skin at the anal margin [MS-6]. Lymphatic drainage patterns differ based on tumor location: distal tumors drain mainly to superficial inguinal nodes, while proximal tumors drain to perirectal and internal iliac nodes, though overlap occurs [MS-7].

subtypes.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)
subtypes.description: The most common histologic type of primary anal cancer. The WHO 5th edition classification includes various histologic patterns (previously designated as cloacogenic, basaloid, or transitional) under a single diagnosis of SCC, as they share similar natural history and prognosis. Distinction by cell type is not made in these guidelines [MS-7].
subtypes.frequency: Most primary cancers of the anal canal are of squamous cell histology.
subtypes.key_associations: Strongly associated with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, particularly HPV-16 and HPV-18 [MS-3].

subtypes.name: Verrucous Carcinoma (VC)
subtypes.description: A low-grade subtype of SCC characterized by marked exophytic and endophytic growth without features of HPV cytopathic effect or high-grade cytologic atypia on superficial biopsy. When conventional SCC is identified arising in VC, the diagnosis should be SCC as these lesions have similar biology [MS-7].
subtypes.frequency: Rare; exact frequency not specified in the source.
subtypes.key_associations: Challenging to diagnose definitively on superficial biopsy [MS-7].

subtypes.name: Superficially Invasive Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SISCCA)
subtypes.description: A clinical-pathologic entity defined as anal cancer that has been completely excised, with ≤3-mm basement membrane invasion and a maximal horizontal spread of ≤7 mm (T1,NX). These are generally found incidentally in biopsies or excisions of benign lesions [ANAL-A; MS-16].
subtypes.frequency: Seen with increasing frequency due to increased anal cancer screening [MS-16].
subtypes.key_associations: May be adequately treated with local excision alone with a structured surveillance plan in carefully selected patients [ANAL-A].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The key molecular event in anal squamous cell carcinoma is infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV), which is detected in 84% to 97% of anal cancer specimens [MS-2, MS-3]. The association with persistent infection by high-risk HPV forms, especially HPV-16 and HPV-18, is especially strong. A study found high-risk HPV DNA in 84% of anal cancer specimens (HPV-16 in 73%), while it was absent in rectal adenocarcinomas [MS-3]. The viral oncoproteins E6 and E7 from high-risk HPV types inactivate the tumor suppressor proteins p53 and pRb, respectively, leading to genomic instability and carcinogenesis. The guidelines do not detail specific frequencies of other driver mutations or chromosomal abnormalities beyond this HPV-driven pathway. Immunosuppression, as seen in HIV infection or after solid organ transplantation, is a critical co-factor that likely facilitates persistence of HPV infection [MS-3]. There is evidence that HPV- and/or p16-positivity are prognostic for improved overall survival, and p16-positivity was an independent prognostic factor for OS in one study (HR, 0.07; P = .016) [MS-8].

epidemiology.incidence: An estimated 10,930 new cases (3,560 in males, 7,370 in females) of cancer involving the anus, anal canal, or anorectum were projected in the United States in 2025, accounting for approximately 3% of digestive system cancers [MS-2]. The incidence rate of invasive anal carcinoma in the U.S. increased by approximately 1.9-fold for males and 1.5-fold for females between the periods 1973–1979 and 1994–2000 and has continued to increase since that time. An analysis of SEER data showed the incidence of anal squamous carcinoma increased at a rate of 2.9% per year from 1992 to 2001. Another analysis reported an annual increase of 2.7% between 2001 and 2015, with the greatest increases in age groups ≥50 years [MS-2].
epidemiology.mortality: An estimated 2,030 deaths due to anal cancer were projected to occur in the United States in 2025 [MS-2]. Anal cancer mortality rates (2001–2016) rose with an average increase of 3.1% per year [MS-2].
epidemiology.trends: The incidence has been steadily increasing over several decades. The increase in lymph node positivity on imaging over time is attributed partly to the use of more sensitive imaging techniques (the 'Will Rogers effect'), not necessarily a shift to more advanced stage at diagnosis [MS-9].
epidemiology.demographics: The disease is more common in females. Increases in incidence were especially noted for females ≥50 years of age [MS-2].

risk_factors.detail: Persistent infection with high-risk HPV (e.g., HPV-16, HPV-18) is the primary risk factor. High-risk HPV DNA is detected in 84%-97% of anal cancers [MS-3].

People with HIV (PWH) have an approximately 15- to 35-fold increased likelihood of being diagnosed with anal cancer compared to the general population. The standardized incidence rate in the U.S. increased from 19.0 per 100,000 person-years (1992-1995) to 78.2 (2000-2003). Incidence is highest in men who have sex with men (MSM) with HIV (131 per 100,000 person-years) [MS-3].
Incidence rates rise with age and years since transplant. Rates can be as high as 24.5 and 29.6 per 100,000 person-years in males and females ≥10 years post-transplant, respectively [MS-3].

risk_factors.factor: Receptive anal intercourse / sexually transmitted diseases
risk_factors.detail: A history of receptive anal intercourse or sexually transmitted disease is a known risk factor [MS-3].
Smoking is listed as an associated risk factor [MS-3].
Incidence rates of 10, 6, and 3 per 100,000 person-years have been reported for patients with systemic lupus erythematosus, ulcerative colitis, and Crohn’s disease, respectively [MS-3].
A history of cervical, vulvar, or vaginal cancer, likely due to shared HPV risk [MS-3].
Multivariate analyses have identified male sex as an independent prognostic factor for worse disease-free survival and overall survival [MS-8].

protective_factors: HPV vaccination (quadrivalent or 9-valent vaccine). A substudy showed 77.5% observed efficacy for the quadrivalent vaccine in preventing high-grade anal intraepithelial neoplasia in men who have sex with men [MS-5].
Antiretroviral therapy (ART) for HIV: Some evidence suggests prolonged ART (>24 months) may be associated with a decrease in the incidence of high-grade anal intraepithelial neoplasia [MS-4].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Anal carcinoma typically presents with rectal bleeding (approximately 45% of patients), pain, or the sensation of a rectal mass (approximately 30% of patients). The majority of primary cancers of the anal canal are of squamous cell histology. Superficially invasive squamous cell carcinoma (SISCCA) are anal cancers that are generally found incidentally in the setting of a biopsy or excision of what is thought to be a benign lesion such as a condyloma, hemorrhoid, or anal skin tag. Perianal squamous cell carcinomas are more likely than those of the anal canal to be well-differentiated and keratinizing large-cell types. The functional anal canal is approximately 3 to 5 cm in length, with its superior border defined as the palpable upper border of the anal sphincter and puborectalis muscles of the anorectal ring, and its inferior border starting at the anal verge, the lowermost edge of the sphincter muscles, corresponding to the introitus of the anal orifice. The perianal region starts at the anal verge and includes the perianal skin over a 5-cm radius from the squamous mucocutaneous junction.

symptoms.name: Rectal bleeding
symptoms.detail: The most common presenting symptom, reported in approximately 45% of patients with anal carcinoma
symptoms.alarm: Yes - warrants investigation for anal or colorectal malignancy

symptoms.name: Rectal pain
symptoms.detail: Approximately 30% of patients present with pain, which may be perianal or related to the anal mass

symptoms.name: Rectal mass sensation
symptoms.detail: Approximately 30% of patients report a sensation of a rectal mass

symptoms.name: Perianal skin lesion
symptoms.detail: Perianal cancers may present as discrete lesions arising from the perianal skin that can be visualized completely when the buttocks are gently pressed
symptoms.alarm: Yes - any perianal lesion should be biopsied

signs.name: Palpable anal mass on digital rectal examination (DRE)
signs.detail: DRE is a critical component of the initial evaluation; T stage is primarily determined through clinical examination including DRE

signs.name: Inguinal lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Inguinal lymph node evaluation is mandatory; synchronous inguinal node metastasis was observed in 6.4% of patients with T1/T2 tumors and 16% of patients with T3/T4 tumors

signs.name: Perianal skin abnormality
signs.detail: Visible or palpable abnormality in the perianal region extending up to 5 cm from the squamous mucocutaneous junction

signs.name: Anal canal mass on anoscopy
signs.detail: Anoscopic examination is part of the standard workup to visualize the anal canal and obtain biopsy

atypical_or_missed: SISCCA found incidentally in the setting of biopsy or excision of what is thought to be a benign lesion such as condyloma, hemorrhoid, or anal skin tag - these are being seen with increasing frequency because anal cancer screening in high-risk populations is becoming more common
Tumors involving both the anal canal and perianal region, making it difficult to distinguish between anal canal and perianal squamous cell carcinoma

High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL) of the anal canal that may be present concomitantly with perianal cancers - important to look for anal canal involvement when conservative management is being considered
Pelvic nodal metastases that are often <0.5 cm, suggesting that routine radiologic evaluation with CT and FDG-PET/CT scan may not be reliable in determining lymph node involvement
Anal canal tumors that cannot be entirely seen when the buttocks are gently pressed (per AJCC 9th edition definition)

red_flags: Rectal bleeding requiring urgent investigation to exclude anal carcinoma, particularly in patients with risk factors such as HPV infection, HIV, immunosuppression, or history of receptive anal intercourse
Palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy requiring biopsy or fine-needle aspiration (FNA) if suspicious nodes are detected
Any perianal or anal lesion requiring biopsy to exclude squamous cell carcinoma or other malignancy
New anal symptoms in patients with HIV or other immunosuppression given 15- to 35-fold increased likelihood of anal cancer diagnosis
Progressive disease on serial examinations following primary chemoradiation treatment - biopsy-proven disease progression indicates need for further intensive treatment
Persistent disease that continues to progress rather than regress during the observation period following completion of radiation and chemotherapy
Symptomatic patients with metastatic disease who may benefit from palliative radiation therapy to the primary site

diagnostic.rationale: Required for histologic confirmation of squamous cell carcinoma; definitive diagnosis; all primary cancers of the anal canal are of squamous cell histology in most cases
diagnostic.key_findings: Squamous cell carcinoma histology; differentiation grade (well, moderately, poorly differentiated); presence of keratinizing vs non-keratinizing features; SISCCA characterized by ≤3-mm basement membrane invasion and maximal horizontal spread of ≤7 mm

diagnostic.rationale: Essential component of the clinical evaluation; T stage is primarily performed through clinical examination
diagnostic.key_findings: Assessment of tumor size, location, and extent of local disease; determination of tumor invasion into adjacent structures

diagnostic.rationale: Allows direct visualization of the anal canal and perianal region; facilitates biopsy
diagnostic.key_findings: Visualization of tumor extent, relationship to anal verge and dentate line; assessment of mucosal involvement

diagnostic.rationale: Mandatory evaluation of regional lymph nodes; inguinal node metastasis is common especially with distal anal cancers
diagnostic.key_findings: Clinical or radiologic enlargement of inguinal nodes; FNA or excisional biopsy of suspicious nodes to confirm metastatic disease

staging.test: Chest/abdomen CT with IV and oral contrast + pelvis CT or MRI with contrast
staging.rationale: Standard staging imaging for all patients; CT should be with IV and oral contrast; pelvis MRI with contrast provides superior soft tissue resolution for pelvic disease assessment
staging.indication: All patients with newly diagnosed anal carcinoma (ANAL-1 and ANAL-2 workup)

staging.rationale: Can be considered to verify staging before treatment; reported to be useful in evaluation of pelvic nodes even with normal-sized lymph nodes on CT; pooled sensitivity and specificity for detection of lymph node involvement reported as 93% and 76% respectively in a recent meta-analysis; FDG-PET/CT led to upstaging in 5% to 38% of patients and downstaging in 8% to 27% of patients; treatment plan modifications occurred in 12% to 59% of patients based on FDG-PET/CT results
staging.indication: Consider in all patients as part of staging workup; performed skull base to mid-thigh; does not replace diagnostic CT

staging.rationale: Provides superior soft tissue resolution compared to CT; recommended for pelvic staging
staging.indication: All patients; can be used instead of pelvis CT; particularly valuable for defining local tumor extent

staging.rationale: Intravenous contrast is not required for the chest CT; evaluates for pulmonary metastases since veins of the anal region are part of the venous network associated with systemic circulation
staging.indication: All patients for distant metastasis evaluation

biomarkers.rationale: Risk of anal carcinoma is 15- to 35-fold increased in people with HIV (PWH); approximately 13% of people in the United States infected with HIV are not aware of their infection status; identification of HIV infection has the potential to improve clinical outcomes
biomarkers.when: If HIV status is unknown; CDC recommends HIV screening for all individuals in all health care settings (opt-out screening)

biomarkers.rationale: HPV- and/or p16-positivity are prognostic for improved overall survival in patients with anal carcinoma; in a retrospective study of 143 tumor samples, p16-positivity was an independent prognostic factor for OS (HR, 0.07; 95% CI, 0.01–0.61; P = .016); high-risk HPV DNA detected in 84% of anal cancer specimens in one study
biomarkers.when: Prognostic assessment; not currently required for treatment decisions

biomarkers.rationale: MSI is uncommon in anal cancer; however, responses to PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors occur in 20% to 24% of patients regardless of MSI status; anal cancers may respond due to high PD-L1 expression and/or high tumor mutational load despite being microsatellite stable
biomarkers.when: MSI/MMR testing is not required per NCCN panel

biomarkers.rationale: FDA added a Boxed warning to the capecitabine label recommending testing for DPYD genetic variants prior to initiating capecitabine unless immediate treatment is necessary

do_not_miss: Biopsy of any suspicious anal or perianal lesion - mandatory for diagnosis
Inguinal lymph node evaluation (palpation and FNA/biopsy of suspicious nodes) - critical for staging
HIV testing if status unknown - affects management and prognosis
Gynecologic examination including cervical cancer screening - due to association with HPV
Fertility risk discussion and counseling before treatment initiation
Assessment of tumor relationship to sphincter muscles for treatment planning
avoid: FDG-PET/CT should not replace diagnostic CT
FDG-PET/CT should not be used as part of surveillance strategy for persistent disease due to concerns for false-positivity from local inflammation leading to unnecessary surgeries
Rectal ultrasound to determine depth of tumor invasion is not used in the staging of anal cancer

t_stages.description: Tumor less than or equal to 2 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor greater than 2 cm but less than or equal to 5 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor greater than 5 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor of any size invades adjacent organ(s), such as the vagina, urethra, or bladder

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
No tumor involvement of regional lymph node(s)
Tumor involvement of regional lymph node(s)
Tumor involvement of inguinal, mesorectal, superior rectal, internal iliac, or obturator lymph node(s)
Tumor involvement of external iliac lymph node(s)
Tumor involvement of N1b (external iliac) with any N1a node(s)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Small tumor (≤2 cm) limited to the anal canal without lymph node involvement or distant metastasis
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; for anal canal: chemoRT; for perianal: local excision if well/moderately differentiated T1N0 or select T2N0 not involving sphincter

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor >2 cm but ≤5 cm without lymph node involvement or distant metastasis
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; chemoRT for anal canal; for perianal: local excision if select T2N0 not involving sphincter, otherwise chemoRT

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Small to moderate tumor with regional lymph node involvement but no distant metastasis
Large tumor (>5 cm) with or without regional lymph node involvement, no distant metastasis
Tumor invading adjacent organs (vagina, urethra, bladder) without regional lymph node involvement or distant metastasis
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Regional disease: 60% five-year survival; worst prognosis among node-negative groups

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invading adjacent organs with regional lymph node involvement, no distant metastasis
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Regional disease: 60% five-year survival; worst prognosis category

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Any primary tumor with distant metastasis
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative; systemic therapy; chemoRT to primary site may be considered for local control

staging_pearls: The AJCC 9th edition removed the former N2 and N3 categories by locations of positive nodes and introduced new categories N1a, N1b, and N1c based on specific nodal regions
N1a represents metastasis in inguinal, mesorectal, superior rectal, internal iliac, or obturator nodes; N1b represents external iliac nodes; N1c represents external iliac with any N1a nodes
Both anal canal and perianal tumors use the AJCC anus staging system (previously perianal cancer used the skin cancer staging system)
Prognosis is related to tumor size and lymph node metastases; male sex, positive lymph nodes, and tumor size >5 cm are independently prognostic for worse OS
The same staging system applies to both anal canal and perianal cancers to avoid undertreatment of perianal cancers that may involve the anal canal
T stage is primarily determined through clinical examination (DRE, anoscopy) rather than pathologic staging since primary treatment does not involve surgical excision
Para-aortic lymph nodes that can be included in a radiation field may be treated with definitive chemoRT, although only limited retrospective data support this use

Distal anal cancers present with a higher incidence of inguinal node metastases, but inguinal node metastases can occur in proximal anal cancer as well since lymphatic drainage systems are not isolated from each other
Five-year survival by stage: localized 80%, regional 60%, distant 30.5%

management_principles.overview: Anal carcinoma is primarily managed with concurrent chemoradiation (chemoRT), a paradigm established after Nigro and colleagues observed complete tumor regression with preoperative 5-fluorouracil (5-FU)–based concurrent chemotherapy and radiation in 1974 [122]. Prior to this, patients with invasive anal carcinoma were routinely treated with abdominoperineal resection (APR); however, local recurrence rates were high, 5-year survival was only 40% to 70%, and morbidity with permanent colostomy was considerable [15]. The current treatment philosophy is organ-preserving: definitive chemoRT achieves cure in the majority of patients while avoiding permanent colostomy. All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated, reflecting uniform NCCN consensus based on lower-level evidence. Clinical trial participation is encouraged when applicable and available [NCCN]. The NCCN Guidelines Panel believes that a multidisciplinary approach including physicians from gastroenterology, medical oncology, surgical oncology, radiation oncology, and radiology is necessary for treating individuals with anal carcinoma [MS-23]. Surgery (APR) is reserved for biopsy-proven recurrent or persistent disease following primary treatment, or for the small subset of very early lesions amenable to local excision. Systemic therapy is the backbone of treatment for metastatic disease, with recent evidence supporting the addition of immune checkpoint inhibitors to first-line chemotherapy [234].

by_intent.patient_group: Non-metastatic anal canal cancer (stage I–III, including positive para-aortic lymph nodes that can be included in the radiation field)
by_intent.approach: Concurrent chemoRT with mitomycin/5-FU, mitomycin/capecitabine, or cisplatin/5-FU (category 2B) [ANAL-1, ANAL-B 1 of 5]

by_intent.patient_group: T1N0 well or moderately differentiated or select T2N0 perianal cancer not involving the sphincter
by_intent.approach: Local excision with 1-cm margins; if margins adequate, observe; if margins inadequate, re-excision preferred or consider local RT ± chemotherapy [ANAL-2, ANAL-A]

by_intent.patient_group: T1N0 poorly differentiated or T2–T4 N0 or any T N+ perianal cancer
by_intent.approach: Local excision → re-excision (preferred) if inadequate margins, or consider local RT ± chemotherapy; OR definitive chemoRT as for anal canal cancer [ANAL-2]

First-line systemic therapy (preferred: carboplatin/paclitaxel + retifanlimab-dlwr); chemo/RT to the primary site may be considered for local control of symptomatic bulky primary [ANAL-1, ANAL-B 2 of 5, ANAL-C 4 of 6]

by_intent.patient_group: Locally recurrent or persistent disease after prior chemoRT
by_intent.approach: APR with colostomy; immunotherapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, retifanlimab-dlwr, cemiplimab-rwlc, dostarlimab-gxly, tislelizumab-jsgr, toripalimab-tpzi, or penpulimab-kcqx) (category 2B) may be considered before proceeding to APR based on institutional experience [ANAL-3, ANAL-4]

management_principles.mdt_requirements: A multidisciplinary approach including physicians from gastroenterology, medical oncology, surgical oncology, radiation oncology, and radiology is necessary for treating individuals with anal carcinoma [MS-23]. The oncologist and primary care provider should have defined roles in the surveillance period, with roles communicated to the patient [MS-23]. Modifications to cancer treatment should not be made solely based on HIV status; additional considerations for people with HIV (PWH) who have anal cancer are outlined in the NCCN Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV, including the use of normal tissue-sparing radiation techniques, the consideration of non-malignant causes for lymphadenopathy, and the need for more frequent post-treatment surveillance anoscopy [ANAL-1 footnote i, MS-16].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: The Panel notes that poor performance status in PWH and anal cancer may be from HIV, cancer, or other causes. The reason for poor performance status should be considered when making treatment decisions. Treatment with antiretroviral therapy (ART) may improve poor performance status related to HIV [MS-16]. For individuals with anal cancer who are not candidates for intensive therapy, the optimal chemotherapy regimen remains uncertain. Some NCCN Panel members have used a combination of weekly cisplatin and daily 5-FU on days of radiation for chemoRT in localized anal cancer. Other potential strategies may include capecitabine plus RT or RT alone (without chemotherapy). However, due to a lack of data supporting this approach and differing strategies among Panel members, recommendations have not yet been defined. Use of a geriatric assessment to guide management and elicitation of the patient's goals and objectives with regard to their cancer diagnosis is critical to inform shared decision-making [MS-15, MS-16].

management_pathways.title: Anal Canal Cancer — Locoregional Disease (± Positive Para-Aortic Lymph Nodes)
branching_basis: Nodal involvement (including para-aortic nodes that can be included in radiation field)

nodes.criteria: Anal canal cancer, locoregional disease (± positive para-aortic lymph nodes that can be included in a radiation field) [ANAL-1]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant chemotherapy is recommended after primary chemoRT for non-metastatic anal canal cancer. The ACT II trial demonstrated that maintenance chemotherapy (5-FU and cisplatin) following chemoRT does not decrease the rate of disease recurrence [143].

management_pathways.title: Perianal Cancer — T1N0 Well/Moderately Differentiated or Select T2N0 (Not Involving Sphincter)

nodes.criteria: T1, N0 well or moderately differentiated or select T2, N0 perianal (anal margin) cancer that does not involve the sphincter [ANAL-2]

options.label: Local excision with adequate margins (1 cm)
Consider local RT ± fluorouracil/mitomycin or capecitabine/mitomycin or cisplatin/fluorouracil (category 2B)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Local excision is adequate for select early perianal lesions. A 1-cm margin is recommended [ANAL-A]. Many patients with T1 or selected T2 perianal cancers will have concomitant HSIL of the anal canal; it is important to look for such anal canal involvement when conservative management is being considered [MS-16].

management_pathways.title: Perianal Cancer — T1N0 Poorly Differentiated, T2–T4 N0, or Any T N+ (± Para-Aortic Nodes)

nodes.criteria: T1, N0 poorly differentiated or T2–T4, N0 or any T, N+ perianal cancer (± positive para-aortic lymph nodes that can be included in radiation field) [ANAL-2]

options.label: Local excision → Re-excision (preferred) if margins inadequate
Consider local RT ± fluorouracil/mitomycin or capecitabine/mitomycin or cisplatin/fluorouracil (category 2B)

adjuvant_logic.summary: For more advanced perianal cancers, treatment options are the same as for anal canal cancer. No adjuvant chemotherapy after chemoRT [ANAL-2, MS-17].

management_pathways.title: Metastatic Anal Carcinoma — First-Line Systemic Therapy

nodes.criteria: Metastatic anal carcinoma, first-line therapy, treatment-naïve [ANAL-B 2 of 5]

adjuvant_logic.summary: For patients with newly diagnosed metastatic anal cancer who present with synchronous local and metastatic disease, chemoRT to the primary site can be considered for local control following first-line chemotherapy [MS-15, ANAL-C 4 of 6].

nodes.criteria: Metastatic disease, chemo/RT to primary site for local control [ANAL-B 2 of 5]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative chemoRT to the primary site can be administered following upfront chemotherapy for local control of a symptomatic bulky primary [MS-20, ANAL-C 4 of 6].

management_pathways.title: Metastatic Anal Carcinoma — Second-Line and Subsequent Systemic Therapy

nodes.criteria: Metastatic anal carcinoma, second-line and subsequent therapy, no prior immunotherapy received [ANAL-B 2 of 5]

adjuvant_logic.summary: MSI/MMR testing is not required. MSI is uncommon in anal cancer, and responses to PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors occur in 20% to 24% of patients [247, 248]. Anal cancers may be responsive to PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors because they often have high PD-L1 expression and/or a high tumor mutational load despite being microsatellite stable (MSS) [253].

nodes.criteria: Metastatic anal carcinoma, second-line and subsequent therapy, if not previously given [ANAL-B 2 of 5]

adjuvant_logic.summary: The Panel includes chemotherapy agents as options in second-line therapy if not previously given in first line, but notes that platinum-based chemotherapy should not be given in second line if disease progressed on platinum-based therapy in first line [MS-22].

nodes.criteria: Complete remission after primary chemoRT, evaluated at 8–12 weeks with exam and DRE [ANAL-3]

options.label: DRE every 3–6 months for 5 years; inguinal node palpation every 3–6 months for 5 years; anoscopy every 6–12 months × 3 years; C/A/P CT with contrast or chest CT without contrast and abdomen/pelvis MRI with contrast annually for 3 years (stage II–III)

adjuvant_logic.summary: FDG-PET/CT is not indicated in routine surveillance after treatment [ANAL-3]. Patients can be classified as having complete remission without biopsy verification if clinical evidence of disease is absent [MS-18].

management_pathways.title: Post-Chemoradiation — Persistent Disease Management
branching_basis: Progression on serial exams vs regression/no progression

nodes.criteria: Persistent disease at initial 8–12 week evaluation [ANAL-4]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Based on the results of the ACT-II study, it may be appropriate to follow patients who have not achieved a complete clinical response with persistent anal cancer up to 6 months following completion of RT and chemotherapy as long as there is no evidence of progressive disease during this period of follow-up. Persistent disease may continue to regress even at 26 weeks from the start of treatment [2, MS-18].

nodes.criteria: Persistent disease with progression on serial exams [ANAL-4]

options.label: Biopsy proven → Restage → Locally recurrent: APR + groin dissection if positive inguinal nodes; Metastatic: First-line Systemic Therapy

adjuvant_logic.summary: Evidence of progression found on DRE should be followed by biopsy as well as restaging with CT and/or FDG-PET/CT imaging [MS-19, ANAL-4].

nodes.criteria: Persistent disease with regression or no progression on serial exams [ANAL-4]

options.label: Continue observation and re-evaluate at 3-month intervals; surveillance (ANAL-3)

adjuvant_logic.summary: If regression or no progression, continue observation with re-evaluation at 3-month intervals. Persistent disease may continue to regress for up to 6 months from the start of treatment, and APR can thereby be avoided in some patients [MS-18, ANAL-4].

management_pathways.title: Locally Recurrent or Progressive Disease After Primary ChemoRT
branching_basis: Site of recurrence (primary site vs inguinal nodes)

options.label: Abdominoperineal resection (APR) with colostomy; consider muscle flap reconstruction
Consider immunotherapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, retifanlimab-dlwr, cemiplimab-rwlc, dostarlimab-gxly, tislelizumab-jsgr, toripalimab-tpzi, or penpulimab-kcqx) (category 2B) before proceeding to APR

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider the use of immunotherapy before proceeding to APR (category 2B). Institutional experience has demonstrated that some patients receive a good response and can avoid surgery [ANAL-3 footnote p]. Muscle flap reconstruction should be considered [ANAL-3 footnote q].

options.label: First-line Systemic Therapy if metastatic disease present

adjuvant_logic.summary: For patients who have already received groin radiation, consider an inguinal node dissection [ANAL-A]. Groin dissection can be done with or without APR depending on whether disease is isolated to the groin or is in conjunction with recurrence/persistence at the primary site [MS-19]. Palliative RT may be considered in symptomatic patients [ANAL-3 footnote o].

management_pathways.title: Metastatic Anal Carcinoma — Chemo/RT to Primary Site for Local Control

nodes.criteria: Previously untreated anal cancer presenting with synchronous local and metastatic disease [ANAL-C 4 of 6, MS-15]

adjuvant_logic.summary: If performance status is good and metastatic disease is limited, treat involved fields, 45–54 Gy to the primary tumor and involved sites in the pelvis, in coordination with plans for a platinum-based regimen. If there is low-volume liver oligometastasis, an SBRT dosing schema after systemic therapy may be appropriate depending on response. If metastatic disease is extensive and life expectancy is limited, a different schedule and dose of radiation should be considered [ANAL-C 4 of 6].

items.recommendation: Required for all patients [ANAL-1, ANAL-2]

items.text: Inguinal lymph node evaluation with palpation
items.recommendation: Required for all patients [ANAL-1, ANAL-2]

items.text: Consider biopsy or fine needle aspiration (FNA) if suspicious inguinal nodes
items.recommendation: Recommended if clinically or radiologically suspicious nodes are identified [ANAL-1, ANAL-2]
Required for all patients [ANAL-1, ANAL-2]

items.text: Biopsy confirming squamous cell carcinoma histology
items.recommendation: Required for diagnosis. For melanoma histology, see NCCN Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous; for adenocarcinoma, see NCCN Guidelines for Rectal Cancer [ANAL-1, ANAL-2]

HPV- and/or p16-positivity are prognostic for improved OS in patients with anal carcinoma. A retrospective study found p16-positivity was an independent prognostic factor for OS (HR, 0.07; 95% CI, 0.01–0.61; P = .016) [105, MS-8]
MSI/MMR testing is not required for treatment decisions. MSI is uncommon in anal cancer [253, MS-22]
For information on DPYD testing and fluoropyrimidine-associated toxicity, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer [ANAL-1 footnote c, ANAL-B 1 of 5 footnote a]
CT should be with IV and oral contrast. Intravenous contrast is not required for the chest CT [ANAL-1, ANAL-2 footnote e]

Pelvis MRI with contrast. If intravenous iodinated contrast material is contraindicated due to significant contrast allergy or renal failure, then MRI examination of the abdomen and pelvis with IV gadolinium-based contrast agent (GBCA) can be obtained in select patients [ANAL-1, ANAL-2 footnote e]

FDG-PET/CT scan does not replace a diagnostic CT. FDG-PET/CT performed skull base to mid-thigh. FDG-PET/CT is useful in the evaluation of pelvic nodes, even in patients with normal-sized lymph nodes on CT imaging [ANAL-1, ANAL-2 footnote f; 112-117]

HIV testing should be performed because the risk of anal carcinoma is higher in people with HIV (PWH). About 13% of people in the United States infected with HIV are not aware of their infection status [ANAL-1, MS-9; 108]
Standard pre-treatment laboratory assessment

FDA added a Boxed warning to the capecitabine label, recommending to test patients for genetic variants of DPYD prior to initiating capecitabine unless immediate treatment is necessary [ANAL-1 footnote c]

items.text: Gynecologic exam, including screening for cervical cancer
items.recommendation: Suggested due to the association of anal cancer and HPV [ANAL-1, ANAL-2; 13]

items.text: Fertility risk discussion/counseling in appropriate patients
items.recommendation: Patients of childbearing potential should be counseled that an irradiated uterus cannot carry a fetus to term. Information regarding sperm banking or oocyte, egg, or ovarian tissue banking should be provided prior to treatment [ANAL-1, ANAL-2, ANAL-C 5 of 6]
Refer to the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health [ANAL-1 footnote d, ANAL-2 footnote d]

items.text: Gynecologic exam for vaginal dilator counseling
items.recommendation: Patients should be considered for vaginal dilators and instructed on the symptoms of vaginal stenosis [ANAL-C 5 of 6]
Critical for treatment planning, particularly in PWH where poor performance status may be from HIV, cancer, or other causes [MS-16]
Use of a geriatric assessment to guide management and elicitation of the patient's goals and objectives is critical for shared decision-making [MS-15]

surgery.role: Surgery plays a limited but critical role in the management of anal carcinoma. The primary treatment for most anal canal cancers is organ-preserving concurrent chemoRT; surgery is reserved for (1) very early perianal lesions amenable to local excision, (2) biopsy-proven recurrent or persistent disease after primary chemoRT (APR), and (3) inguinal node recurrence (groin dissection) [MS-15, MS-19]. Radical surgery (APR) is the primary treatment for local recurrence/persistence following failed chemoRT [ANAL-A].
principles: Local excision is used for SISCCA and select early perianal cancers with histologically negative margins [ANAL-A]
For SISCCA, local excision alone with a structured surveillance plan may represent adequate treatment in carefully selected patients followed by an experienced provider and/or team [ANAL-A, MS-16]
APR for anal cancer may require wider lateral perianal margins than are required for rectal cancer [ANAL-A, MS-19]
General principles for APR are similar to those for distal rectal cancer and include the incorporation of total mesorectal excision (TME) [ANAL-A, MS-19]
Reconstructive tissue flaps (vertical rectus or local myocutaneous flaps) should be considered due to poor perineal wound healing following prior RT [ANAL-A, MS-19; 227, 228]
For patients who have already received groin radiation, consider an inguinal node dissection [ANAL-A]
Groin dissection can be done with or without APR depending on whether disease is isolated to the groin or is in conjunction with recurrence/persistence at the primary site [ANAL-A, MS-19]

procedures.name: Local Excision (for SISCCA)
procedures.indication: Superficially invasive squamous cell carcinoma (SISCCA): anal cancer that has been completely excised, with ≤3-mm basement membrane invasion and a maximal horizontal spread of ≤7 mm (T1,NX). Generally found incidentally in the setting of biopsy or excision of a benign lesion such as a condyloma, hemorrhoid, or anal skin tag [ANAL-A, 194]
procedures.key_complications: Local recurrence risk; HSIL detected in 74% of patients following local excision in observational studies [195]
procedures.operative_mortality: Not reported specifically for this procedure

procedures.name: Local Excision (for Perianal Cancer)
procedures.indication: T1N0, well or moderately differentiated or select T2N0 squamous cell carcinoma of the perianal (anal margin) region. Where the tumor forms a discrete lesion arising from the perianal skin clearly separate from the anal canal, where negative margin excision can be accomplished without compromise of adjacent sphincter muscles, and where there is no evidence of regional nodal involvement [ANAL-A, MS-16]
procedures.operative_mortality: Not reported specifically for this procedure

procedures.name: Abdominoperineal Resection (APR)
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive surgical resection with colostomy, incorporating TME
procedures.indication: Biopsy-proven locally recurrent or persistent disease following primary chemoRT [ANAL-3, ANAL-4, MS-19]
procedures.key_complications: Perineal wound healing complications (especially after prior RT; rectus abdominis myocutaneous flap reconstruction may decrease complications [227, 228]), sexual dysfunction, urinary dysfunction [MS-19, MS-23]
procedures.operative_mortality: 5-year survival rates of 39% to 66% have been observed in studies involving a minimum of 25 patients; complication rates reported to be high [MS-19; 216, 217, 221-225]

procedures.name: Inguinal Node Dissection
procedures.approach: Open surgical dissection of inguinal lymph nodes
procedures.indication: Inguinal node recurrence; patients who require an APR but have already received groin radiation [ANAL-A, ANAL-3, MS-19]
procedures.operative_mortality: Not reported specifically for this procedure

surgery.resectability_criteria: APR is indicated for biopsy-proven locally recurrent or persistent disease following primary chemoRT [ANAL-3, ANAL-4]. In an attempt to avoid surgery, the use of immunotherapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, retifanlimab-dlwr, cemiplimab-rwlc, dostarlimab-gxly, tislelizumab-jsgr, toripalimab-tpzi, or penpulimab-kcqx) (category 2B) may be considered prior to APR; institutional experience has demonstrated that some patients receive a good response and can avoid surgery [ANAL-3 footnote p, MS-19]. Factors associated with worse prognosis following APR include an initial presentation of node-positive disease and RT doses <55 Gy used in the treatment of primary disease [217].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Reconstructive tissue flaps for the perineum, such as the vertical rectus or local myocutaneous flaps, should be considered for patients with anal cancer undergoing an APR due to the necessary exposure of the perineum to radiation and the resulting poor perineal wound healing [ANAL-A, MS-19]. For patients who have already received groin radiation, consider an inguinal node dissection rather than re-irradiation [ANAL-A]. Consider muscle flap reconstruction [ANAL-3 footnote q].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is a central component of definitive treatment for non-metastatic anal carcinoma, delivered concurrently with chemotherapy (chemoRT). The consensus of the NCCN Panel is that intensity-modulated RT (IMRT) is preferred over 3D conformal RT (3D-CRT) [ANAL-C 1 of 6, 193]. RT is also used for palliation of symptomatic recurrent or metastatic disease, and for oligometastatic disease (SBRT may be considered) [ANAL-C 1 of 6].
principles: IMRT is preferred over 3D-CRT and requires expertise and careful target design to avoid reduction in local control by 'marginal miss' [ANAL-C 1 of 6, 3]
Image-guided RT (IGRT) with kilovoltage (kV) imaging or cone beam CT imaging should be routinely used during treatment with IMRT and SBRT [ANAL-C 1 of 6]
Consider SBRT for patients with oligometastatic disease [ANAL-C 1 of 6]
Avoid extension of overall chemoRT treatment time to improve progression-free survival [ANAL-C 5 of 6]
Fusion of MRI pelvis and/or FDG-PET/CT or FDG-PET/MRI with the simulation CT is recommended in defining local and regional target structures [ANAL-C 1 of 6]
Displacement of the external genitalia from the radiation field should be considered to minimize treatment dose [ANAL-C 1 of 6]
Patients are instructed to maintain a full bladder and, if possible, a rectum empty of stool and gas for simulation and treatment [ANAL-C 1 of 6]
A radiopaque marker should be placed at the anal verge and perianal skin involvement can be outlined with radio-opaque markers [ANAL-C 1 of 6]
The pelvic and inguinal nodes should be routinely treated in all patients [ANAL-C 2 of 6]
For patients with cT3/4 anal cancer, consider selective dose escalation to 53.2–59.4 Gy in 28–33 fractions [ANAL-C 3 of 6]

dose_frameworks.name: Standard Shrinking Field Technique (RTOG 98-11 based)
dose_frameworks.indication: Standard treatment for non-metastatic anal canal cancer. Low-risk elective nodal PTV: 30.6 Gy in 1.8 Gy fractions. High-risk elective nodal PTV: additional 14.4 Gy (total 45 Gy). Gross disease PTV: additional 5.4–14.4 Gy (total 50.4–59.4 Gy) [ANAL-C 3 of 6]

dose_frameworks.name: RTOG 0529 Simultaneous Integrated Boost (SIB)
dose_frameworks.indication: Dose-painted IMRT with SIB technique. T1,N0: Primary 50.4 Gy/28 fx at 1.8 Gy/fx, Nodal 42 Gy/28 fx at 1.5 Gy/fx. T2,N0: same as T1,N0. T3–4,N0: Primary 54 Gy/30 fx at 1.8 Gy/fx, Nodal 45 Gy/30 fx at 1.5 Gy/fx. T any,N+(≤3 cm): Primary 54 Gy/30 fx at 1.8 Gy/fx, Involved nodal 50.4 Gy/30 fx at 1.68 Gy/fx, Nodal 45 Gy/30 fx at 1.5 Gy/fx. T any,N+(>3 cm): Primary 54 Gy/30 fx at 1.8 Gy/fx, Involved nodal 54 Gy/30 fx at 1.8 Gy/fx, Nodal 45 Gy/30 fx at 1.5 Gy/fx [ANAL-C 3 of 6, Table 1]

dose_frameworks.name: Selective Dose Escalation for cT3/4
dose_frameworks.indication: For patients with cT3/4 anal cancer, consider selective dose escalation [ANAL-C 3 of 6]

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT
dose_frameworks.indication: Pure palliation for recurrent disease in previously irradiated fields. RT technique and doses are dependent on dosing and technique of prior treatment [ANAL-C 4 of 6]

approaches.name: IMRT with Concurrent 5-FU/Mitomycin
approaches.dose_fractionation: Per RTOG 0529 or standard shrinking field technique
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Fluorouracil 1000 mg/m2/day continuous infusion Days 1–4 and 29–32 + Mitomycin 10 mg/m2 IV bolus Days 1 and 29 (capped at 20 mg) or Mitomycin 12 mg/m2 IV bolus Day 1 (capped at 20 mg)
approaches.indication: Primary treatment of non-metastatic anal canal cancer
approaches.key_trial: RTOG 0529: Grade 2+ hematologic events reduced (73% vs 85%; P = .032), grade 3+ GI events reduced (21% vs 36%; P = .008), grade 3+ dermatologic events reduced (23% vs 49%; P < .0001) [189]. Long-term: 8-year OS 62%, 8-year DFS 62% [190].
approaches.toxicities: Grade 2+ combined acute adverse event rate was 77%. Grade ≥2 non-hematologic late toxicities rate was 15% [189, 190].

approaches.name: IMRT with Concurrent Capecitabine/Mitomycin
approaches.dose_fractionation: Per RTOG 0529 or standard shrinking field technique
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Capecitabine 825 mg/m2 PO BID Monday–Friday on days of radiation treatment only + Mitomycin 10 mg/m2 IV bolus Days 1 and 29 (capped at 20 mg) or Mitomycin 12 mg/m2 IV bolus Day 1 (capped at 20 mg)
approaches.indication: Primary treatment of non-metastatic anal canal cancer; alternative to 5-FU/mitomycin
approaches.key_trial: Phase II study found 6-month locoregional control rate of 86% (95% CI, 0.72–0.94) [142]. Retrospective study showed significantly lower grade 3/4 hematologic toxicities with capecitabine vs 5-FU [139].
approaches.toxicities: Grade 3/4 hematologic toxicities significantly lower compared to 5-FU-based regimen [139]

approaches.name: IMRT with Concurrent Cisplatin/5-FU
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Cisplatin 75 mg/m2 IV Day 1 + continuous infusion Fluorouracil 1000 mg/m2/day IV Days 1–4, repeat every 4 weeks
approaches.indication: Primary treatment of non-metastatic anal canal cancer (category 2B)
approaches.key_trial: ACT II trial demonstrated equivalence of cisplatin and mitomycin in chemoRT regimens [143]. RTOG 98-11 showed inferior 5-year DFS (57.8% vs 67.8%; P = .006) and 5-year OS (70.7% vs 78.3%; P = .026) for cisplatin-based regimen [100, 144].
approaches.toxicities: Higher toxicity compared to mitomycin-based regimen in RTOG 98-11 [100, 144]

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy serves two roles in anal carcinoma: (1) concurrent chemotherapy with radiation for non-metastatic disease, and (2) palliative systemic therapy for metastatic disease. For localized disease, mitomycin/5-FU or mitomycin/capecitabine with concurrent RT are preferred regimens, with cisplatin/5-FU as a category 2B alternative [ANAL-B 1 of 5]. For metastatic disease, carboplatin/paclitaxel plus the PD-1 inhibitor retifanlimab-dlwr is the preferred first-line regimen based on the phase 3 POD1UM-303/InterAACT2 trial showing improved PFS (9.30 vs 7.39 months; HR = 0.63; P = .0006) [234, ANAL-B 2 of 5]. PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors are preferred options for second-line and subsequent therapy [ANAL-B 2 of 5, MS-21, MS-22].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Localized anal canal cancer — concurrent chemoradiation
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Mitomycin is an important component of chemoRT; the phase III Intergroup study demonstrated lower colostomy rate (9% vs 22%; P = .002) and higher 4-year DFS (73% vs 51%; P = .0003) with 5-FU/mitomycin compared to 5-FU alone [132]. ACT II demonstrated equivalence of cisplatin and mitomycin in chemoRT [143].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: DPYD testing recommended before initiating capecitabine per FDA Boxed Warning [ANAL-B 1 of 5 footnote a]

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase 3 POD1UM-303/InterAACT2 trial: improved median PFS (9.30 vs 7.39 months; HR = 0.63; P = .0006) and favorable OS trend (29.2 vs 23.0 months) [234, MS-20].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: CAPEOX, Carboplatin/Paclitaxel, FOLFCIS, FOLFOX, Cisplatin/Fluorouracil (category 2B), Modified DCF (category 2B) [ANAL-B 2 of 5]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: DPYD testing for fluoropyrimidine-containing regimens [ANAL-B 2 of 5 footnote a]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — first-line, chemo/RT to primary for local control
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: For symptomatic bulky primary tumors in metastatic setting [ANAL-C 4 of 6, MS-20]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: DPYD testing for capecitabine [ANAL-B 2 of 5 footnote a]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — second-line and subsequent (no prior immunotherapy)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cemiplimab-rwlc, Dostarlimab-gxly, Nivolumab, Pembrolizumab, Penpulimab-kcqx, Retifanlimab-dlwr, Tislelizumab-jsgr, or Toripalimab-tpzi
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Response rates of 20–24% with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in previously treated anal cancer [247, 248]. MSI/MMR testing is not required; responses occur even in MSS tumors [253, MS-22].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: CAPEOX, Carboplatin/Paclitaxel, FOLFCIS, FOLFOX, Cisplatin/Fluorouracil (category 2B), Modified DCF (category 2B) if not previously given [ANAL-B 2 of 5]. Platinum-based chemotherapy should not be given in second line if disease progressed on platinum-based therapy in first line [MS-22].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MSI/MMR testing not required. PD-L1 testing not required [MS-22, 253]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Locally recurrent/persistent disease — prior to APR
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Immunotherapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, retifanlimab-dlwr, cemiplimab-rwlc, dostarlimab-gxly, tislelizumab-jsgr, toripalimab-tpzi, or penpulimab-kcqx) (category 2B)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Institutional experience has demonstrated that some patients receive a good response and can avoid surgery [ANAL-3 footnote p]

key_regimens.name: Fluorouracil/Mitomycin + RT (localized)
key_regimens.setting: Localized anal canal cancer — concurrent chemoradiation
key_regimens.cycle_days: Concurrent with RT; repeat 5-FU and mitomycin in week 5
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3/4 hematologic events, dermatologic toxicity, GI toxicity [100, 189]

key_regimens.name: Fluorouracil/Mitomycin + RT — Alternative dosing (localized)
key_regimens.setting: Localized anal canal cancer — concurrent chemoradiation
key_regimens.cycle_days: Concurrent with RT; single mitomycin dose on day 1
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Similar to standard dosing [ANAL-B 1 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Capecitabine/Mitomycin + RT (localized)
key_regimens.setting: Localized anal canal cancer — concurrent chemoradiation

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.day: Monday–Friday, on days of radiation treatment only, throughout duration of RT (typically 28–30 treatment days)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Significantly lower grade 3/4 hematologic toxicities compared to 5-FU-based regimen [139]

key_regimens.name: Capecitabine/Mitomycin + RT — Alternative dosing (localized)
key_regimens.setting: Localized anal canal cancer — concurrent chemoradiation

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.day: Monday–Friday, on days of radiation treatment only, throughout duration of RT

key_regimens.cycle_days: Concurrent with RT; single mitomycin dose on day 1
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Similar to standard capecitabine/mitomycin dosing [ANAL-B 1 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Fluorouracil + RT (localized)
key_regimens.setting: Localized anal canal cancer — concurrent chemoradiation (category 2B)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Category 2B reflects inferior outcomes compared to mitomycin-based regimen in RTOG 98-11 [5, 100, 144]

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Retifanlimab-dlwr (metastatic first-line preferred)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — first-line preferred

drugs.name: Retifanlimab-dlwr
drugs.day: Day 1 (repeated every 28 days for 6 cycles, followed by 500 mg every 28 days for up to 7 cycles)

key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 4 weeks for chemotherapy × 6 cycles + retifanlimab every 28 days
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Improved median PFS (9.30 vs 7.39 months; HR = 0.63; P = .0006); favorable OS trend (29.2 vs 23.0 months) [234, ANAL-B 3 of 5]

key_regimens.name: CAPEOX (metastatic first-line other recommended)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — first-line and second-line if not previously given
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neuropathy from oxaliplatin; discontinuation of oxaliplatin should be strongly considered after 3 to 4 months of therapy (or sooner for unacceptable neurotoxicity) while maintaining other agents until time of progression [ANAL-B 2 of 5 footnote d]

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel (metastatic first-line other recommended)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — first-line and second-line if not previously given
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 21 days [OR: Carboplatin AUC 5 Day 1 + Paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 Days 1, 8, 15, repeat every 28 days]
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Lower toxicity compared to cisplatin/5-FU in InterAACT trial (71% vs 76% grade ≥3 toxicity; 36% vs 62% serious adverse events; P = .016) [236, ANAL-B 3 of 5]

key_regimens.name: FOLFCIS (metastatic first-line other recommended)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — first-line and second-line if not previously given

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus Day 1, then 1000 mg/m2/day × 2 days (total 2000 mg/m2 over 46–48 hours)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Retrospective study showed 48% response rate, PFS 7.1 months, OS 22.1 months [242, ANAL-B 3 of 5]

key_regimens.name: FOLFOX (metastatic first-line other recommended)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — first-line and second-line if not previously given

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus Day 1, then 1200 mg/m2/day × 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46–48 hours)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Discontinuation of oxaliplatin should be strongly considered after 3 to 4 months of therapy (or sooner for unacceptable neurotoxicity) while maintaining other agents until time of progression [ANAL-B 2 of 5 footnote d]

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Fluorouracil (metastatic)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — first-line and second-line if not previously given (category 2B)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Category 2B: similar efficacy but higher toxicity compared to carboplatin/paclitaxel in randomized trial (InterAACT) [11, 236]

key_regimens.name: Modified DCF (metastatic first-line other recommended)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — first-line and second-line if not previously given (category 2B)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: 53% had at least one grade 3–4 adverse event (vs 83% for standard DCF). Most common grade 3–4 AEs: neutropenia, diarrhea, asthenia, anemia, lymphopenia, mucositis, and vomiting [245, ANAL-B 3 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Cemiplimab-rwlc (metastatic second-line preferred)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — second-line and subsequent (no prior immunotherapy)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Based on extrapolation from colon and rectal cancers; no dedicated anal cancer trials [215, 216, MS-22]

key_regimens.name: Dostarlimab-gxly (metastatic second-line preferred)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — second-line and subsequent (no prior immunotherapy)

drugs.name: Dostarlimab-gxly
drugs.day: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then 1000 mg every 6 weeks

key_regimens.cycle_days: 500 mg every 3 weeks × 4 doses, followed by 1000 mg every 6 weeks
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Based on extrapolation from mismatch repair deficient solid tumors [17, ANAL-B 3 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab (metastatic second-line preferred)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — second-line and subsequent (no prior immunotherapy)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Alternative dosing: 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks OR 480 mg every 4 weeks OR 6 mg/kg every 4 weeks. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab [ANAL-B 3 of 5 footnote e]
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Phase 2 trial: response rate 24% (95% CI, 15–33) among 37 evaluable participants; 13% grade 3 adverse events [247, ANAL-B 4 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab (metastatic second-line preferred)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — second-line and subsequent (no prior immunotherapy)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Alternative dosing: 2 mg/kg every 3 weeks OR 400 mg every 6 weeks OR 4 mg/kg every 6 weeks. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [ANAL-B 3 of 5 footnote f]
key_regimens.key_toxicities: KEYNOTE-028: response rate 17% (95% CI, 5–37); 17% grade 3 AEs [248]. KEYNOTE-158: 11% objective response rate in 112 anal cancer patients [249, 250, ANAL-B 4 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Penpulimab-kcqx (metastatic second-line preferred)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — second-line and subsequent (no prior immunotherapy)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Added based on data from nasopharyngeal carcinoma studies; extrapolated to anal cancer [20, ANAL-B 2 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Tislelizumab-jsgr (metastatic second-line preferred)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — second-line and subsequent (no prior immunotherapy)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Based on data in MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors; extrapolated [22-25, ANAL-B 3 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Toripalimab-tpzi (metastatic second-line preferred)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — second-line and subsequent (no prior immunotherapy)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Based on data in MSI-H/dMMR colorectal cancer; extrapolated [26, ANAL-B 3 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Fluorouracil + RT (chemo/RT to primary for local control)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — chemo/RT to primary site for local control
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 3 weeks for 5 weeks with RT
key_regimens.key_toxicities: See Principles of Radiation Therapy (ANAL-C) for chemoRT toxicities [ANAL-B 3 of 5]

key_regimens.name: Capecitabine + RT (chemo/RT to primary for local control)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic anal cancer — chemo/RT to primary site for local control

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.day: Monday–Friday, on days of radiation treatment only, throughout duration of RT (typically 28–30 treatment days)
