treatment_response_assessment.title: Post-Chemoradiation Response Assessment and Follow-Up
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Patients are re-evaluated by DRE between 8 and 12 weeks after completion of chemoRT [ANAL-3, MS-18].

response_logic.definition: Clinical evidence of disease is absent; can be classified without biopsy verification [MS-18]
Residual disease clinically present at 8–12 weeks but without evidence of progression
response_logic.action: Re-evaluate in 4 weeks [ANAL-4]. Based on the ACT-II study, it may be appropriate to follow patients with persistent anal cancer up to 6 months following completion of RT and chemotherapy as long as there is no evidence of progressive disease during this period. Persistent disease may continue to regress even at 26 weeks from the start of treatment [2, MS-18]. If regression or no progression on serial exams, continue observation and re-evaluate at 3-month intervals. If progression, biopsy and restage [ANAL-4].

response_logic.definition: Documented increase in disease on serial clinical exams, biopsy-proven
response_logic.action: Restage with imaging. If locally recurrent → APR with groin dissection if positive inguinal nodes. If metastatic → First-line Systemic Therapy [ANAL-3]. Consider immunotherapy (category 2B) before proceeding to APR [ANAL-3 footnote p].

imaging_recommendations: FDG-PET/CT is not indicated in routine surveillance [ANAL-3, ANAL-4]
C/A/P CT with contrast or chest CT without contrast and abdomen/pelvis MRI with contrast annually for 3 years (stage II–III) [ANAL-3]
Inguinal node palpation every 3–6 months for 5 years [ANAL-3]
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Evidence of progression found on DRE should be followed by biopsy as well as restaging with CT and/or FDG-PET/CT imaging [MS-19, ANAL-4]
Use imaging studies as per initial workup for restaging [ANAL-4 footnote s]

The Panel recommends against the use of FDG-PET/CT imaging as part of the reevaluation strategy for persistent disease due to concerns for false-positivity from local inflammation from RT leading to unnecessary surgeries [MS-18]
Patients with biopsy-proven locally progressive disease are candidates for radical surgery with an APR and colostomy [MS-19]

prognosis.overall: Anal carcinoma prognosis is variable and stage-dependent, with a generally favorable outlook for localized disease but poorer outcomes for advanced or metastatic presentations. The prognosis is influenced by primary tumor size, lymph node status, HIV status, and HPV/p16 expression. Achieving a complete clinical response after primary chemoradiation is the strongest predictor of favorable long-term outcomes.

by_stage.context: Approximately 50% of anal carcinomas are localized at diagnosis.
About 29% of patients present with regional nodal disease.
Approximately 12% of patients present with metastatic disease.

prognostic_factors: Tumor size: Larger tumor size (>5 cm) is an independent adverse prognostic factor for overall survival (OS) and colostomy rate.

Nodal status: Positive lymph nodes are associated with worse disease-free survival (DFS) and OS. Among node-positive patients, survival is related more to T-stage than nodal burden, with 5-year survival rates of 72.7% and 39.9% for T1-T2 vs. T3-T4 tumors.
Sex: Male sex is an independent prognostic factor for worse DFS and OS.
HPV/p16 status: HPV- and/or p16-positivity are associated with improved OS (p16-positive: HR, 0.07; 95% CI, 0.01–0.61; P = .016).

Response to therapy: Complete clinical response (CCR) at 26 weeks post-treatment is strongly associated with superior 5-year survival. Persistent disease may continue to regress up to 6 months post-chemoradiation.

HIV status: While most studies show similar efficacy of chemoradiation in HIV-positive vs. negative patients, some data suggest a greater risk of grade ≥3 cutaneous toxicities (RR = 1.34) and worse 3-year DFS (RR = 1.32) and OS (RR = 1.77) in people living with HIV.
prognosis.prognostic_context: The 5-year survival rates for locoregional recurrence treated with salvage abdominoperineal resection (APR) range from 39% to 66%. Factors associated with worse prognosis after APR include initial node-positive disease and prior RT doses <55 Gy. For metastatic disease, the median OS with first-line carboplatin/paclitaxel ± retifanlimab is approximately 29.2 months with the addition of immunotherapy vs. 23.0 months for chemotherapy alone.

disease_related.management: Biopsy-proven recurrence is treated with salvage abdominoperineal resection (APR). For isolated inguinal node recurrence, inguinal node dissection is recommended. Palliative RT or systemic therapy can be considered for symptomatic control.

disease_related.complication: Distant metastasis (most common sites: liver, lung, extrapelvic nodes)
disease_related.management: Treated with systemic therapy. Palliative RT to the primary site can be considered for local control following upfront chemotherapy.

chemotherapy: Hematologic toxicities: Neutropenia, anemia, lymphopenia, particularly with mitomycin- or platinum-based regimens.
Nausea/vomiting: Common with platinum agents (cisplatin, carboplatin) and mitomycin.
Fluoropyrimidine-associated toxicity: Hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, mucositis. DPYD genetic variants increase risk; testing is recommended per NCCN Colon Cancer guidelines.
immunotherapy: Immune-mediated adverse events (imAEs): Can affect any organ system, including colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, and dermatologic reactions. Management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities.
targeted_therapy: Cetuximab (EGFR inhibitor): Associated with increased toxicity, including rash, infusion reactions, and higher rates of serious adverse events. No longer recommended due to high toxicity (e.g., grade 4 toxicity in 26-32%, treatment-associated death in 4-5% in phase II trials).
surgery: Abdominoperineal Resection (APR): Risk of poor perineal wound healing (especially post-RT), permanent colostomy, and urogenital dysfunction (sexual dysfunction, urinary incontinence). Reconstructive tissue flaps (e.g., vertical rectus abdominis myocutaneous flap) are recommended to improve wound healing.
Local excision: Risk of positive margins if not performed with adequate (1 cm) margins; potential for undertreatment if anal canal HSIL is present.
radiation: Acute toxicity: Proctitis, perineal dermatitis (common at higher doses), enteritis, cystitis, hematologic toxicity. Treatment breaks may be required for toxicity but should be minimized to avoid compromising outcomes.

Late toxicity (chronic): Bowel dysfunction (increased stool frequency, incontinence, urgency, clustering, cramping), sexual dysfunction (dyspareunia, erectile dysfunction, vaginal stenosis, dryness), urinary dysfunction, vaginal stenosis, potential for pelvic fractures (3-fold increased risk in females ≥65), and secondary malignancies.
Infertility: Risk of premature menopause in females and low testosterone in males; counseling on fertility preservation is critical pre-treatment.

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is integral throughout the treatment and survivorship continuum, aiming to manage treatment toxicities, preserve function, and optimize quality of life. A multidisciplinary approach involving oncology, surgery, radiation, gastroenterology, urology, gynecology, and supportive care specialists is essential.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Nutritional status should be monitored, as pelvic radiotherapy can cause malabsorption and diarrhea. Diet manipulation (e.g., low-fiber for diarrhea, bulk-forming agents for incontinence) and potential referral to a nutritionist are recommended. Role of nutritional intervention in managing gastrointestinal symptoms post-pelvic RT is noted.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Antiemetics are used based on the emetogenic potential of the chemotherapy regimen. Platinum-based (cisplatin) and mitomycin regimens are moderately to highly emetogenic and require prophylaxis per antiemetic guidelines.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Growth factor support (G-CSF) may be considered for regimens with high risk of febrile neutropenia or for patients with prior episodes of severe neutropenia.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: VTE prophylaxis should be considered per institutional guidelines for hospitalized patients or those with additional risk factors.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain should be assessed and managed appropriately. This may include analgesics for acute treatment-related pain (e.g., proctitis) and management of chronic pain syndromes.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Distress screening using tools like the NCCN Distress Thermometer is recommended at diagnosis and during follow-up. Support for issues related to body image changes, sexual dysfunction, ostomy management, and fear of recurrence should be provided. Referral to social work, psychology, or support groups is beneficial.
supportive_care.dental_care: Dental evaluation prior to chemoradiation is recommended to address potential oral complications, especially with regimens affecting mucosal integrity.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Following curative-intent chemoradiation, patients are evaluated 8-12 weeks post-treatment with DRE to assess response. Based on the ACT-II study, patients with persistent disease (not complete response) without progression may be observed for up to 6 months, as continued regression can occur. Re-evaluation is performed every 4 weeks until progression or regression/no progression is confirmed. For complete remission, transition to a structured surveillance program is initiated.
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The goal of surveillance is to detect recurrence early, when additional curative-intent treatment (e.g., salvage surgery, re-irradiation) may still be possible. Late recurrences can occur, warranting long-term monitoring. The intensity of surveillance is highest in the first 3-5 years, corresponding to the peak risk period for recurrence.
late_effects_screening: Bowel function: Screen for chronic diarrhea, incontinence, urgency, stool clustering, and cramping.
Urogenital dysfunction: Screen for sexual dysfunction (erectile dysfunction, dyspareunia, vaginal stenosis, dryness), urinary incontinence, frequency, and urgency.
Bone health: Consider bone density monitoring for patients who received pelvic RT, given increased fracture risk.
Second primary cancers: Encourage age- and gender-appropriate cancer screenings (e.g., cervical, breast, colorectal).
Psychosocial and functional: Screen for distress, fatigue, and impacts on quality of life.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence patterns are often locoregional (10-30% locoregional failure rate after primary chemoradiation). Higher T and N stage are associated with higher recurrence rates. Most recurrences occur within the first 3 years, but late recurrences are possible. Metastatic recurrence most commonly involves the liver, lungs, and extrapelvic lymph nodes.

clinical_follow_up_schedule: - Digital rectal examination (DRE) every 3-6 months for 5 years.
- Inguinal lymph node palpation every 3-6 months for 5 years.
- DRE and inguinal node palpation every 3-6 months for 5 years.
- Anoscopy every 6-12 months for 3 years (consider if anal canal not removed).
imaging_strategy: For stage II-III disease after primary treatment:
- Annual chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast OR chest CT without contrast and abdomen/pelvis MRI with contrast for 3 years.
For post-APR or following treatment of recurrence:
- Annual chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast OR chest CT without contrast and abdomen/pelvis MRI with contrast for 3 years.
FDG-PET/CT is NOT routinely indicated for surveillance due to concerns for false-positivity from post-treatment inflammation.
laboratory_monitoring: No specific serum tumor markers are routinely recommended for surveillance.
HIV testing: If HIV status is unknown at diagnosis, testing is recommended. For known HIV-positive patients, management should follow NCCN Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV.
supportive_follow_up: Fertility counseling and discussion of long-term effects of treatment (sexual function, bowel/bladder function).
Referral to pelvic floor rehabilitation if needed for bowel or urinary dysfunction.
Ostomy care coordination and support group referral if applicable.
Bone density monitoring as indicated for post-RT patients.
Smoking cessation counseling per NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation.

special_situations.key_message: Treatment of anal carcinoma should not be modified solely based on HIV status. Most data show comparable efficacy and safety of standard chemoradiation, though some studies report higher rates of severe acute cutaneous toxicity.
special_situations.management: Standard chemoradiation regimens are recommended. Consider normal tissue-sparing radiation techniques (IMRT). More frequent post-treatment surveillance anoscopy may be warranted. Assess whether poor performance status is due to HIV, cancer, or other causes; ART may improve performance status if related to HIV.
special_situations.evidence: Systematic review/meta-analysis showed RR for grade ≥3 cutaneous toxicity 1.34, worse 3-year DFS (RR 1.32) and OS (RR 1.77) in HIV-positive patients. However, many studies show no significant difference in outcomes.

special_situations.key_message: Not all patients with persistent disease immediately post-treatment require salvage surgery. Continued clinical regression can occur for up to 6 months from the start of treatment.
special_situations.management: If no progression on serial exams, continue observation and re-evaluate at 3-month intervals for up to 6 months. Avoid routine FDG-PET/CT due to false-positivity risk. If progression occurs, proceed to salvage surgery (APR).
special_situations.evidence: ACT-II study: 72% of patients without complete response at 11 weeks achieved it by 26 weeks. 5-year survival was superior in patients achieving complete response at 26 weeks.

special_situations.key_message: Salvage surgery is the primary treatment for biopsy-proven local recurrence. Immunotherapy may be considered prior to APR to avoid surgery in selected patients.
special_situations.management: Abdominoperineal resection (APR) with total mesorectal excision (TME). Consider muscle flap reconstruction for perineal wound closure. For isolated inguinal node recurrence after prior RT, inguinal node dissection is recommended. Palliative RT may be considered for symptomatic control.
special_situations.evidence: 5-year survival after salvage APR ranges from 39-66%. Institutional experience suggests some patients may achieve good response to immunotherapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, retifanlimab-dlwr, etc.) before APR, potentially avoiding surgery.

special_situations.key_message: Systemic therapy is the mainstay of treatment. The addition of checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy to chemotherapy has improved outcomes in the first-line setting.
special_situations.management: First-line: Carboplatin/paclitaxel + retifanlimab-dlwr (preferred). Other options: CAPEOX, FOLFCIS, FOLFOX, carboplatin/paclitaxel alone, cisplatin/fluorouracil (2B), modified DCF (2B). Chemo/RT to primary site can be considered for local control. Second-line: Checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, etc.) if not given in first-line; other chemotherapy if not previously given.
special_situations.evidence: POD1UM-303/InterAACT2: Retifanlimab + carboplatin/paclitaxel improved median PFS (9.3 vs. 7.4 months; HR 0.63; P = .0006) and trended for OS (29.2 vs. 23.0 months) vs. chemotherapy alone.

special_situations.key_message: SISCCA, often found incidentally, may be adequately treated with local excision alone if margins are negative, followed by structured surveillance.
special_situations.management: For SISCCA with histologically negative margins, local excision alone with a structured surveillance plan may be sufficient. Requires careful follow-up as HSIL is detected in 74% of patients post-excision.
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective study of 17 patients with completely excised invasive anal cancer: no difference in 5-year OS (100%) or cancer recurrence-free survival (87%) between superficially invasive and invasive groups.

special_situations.key_message: Early-stage perianal cancer not involving the sphincter may be treated with local excision if adequate margins can be achieved.
special_situations.management: Local excision with 1-cm margins. If margins are inadequate, re-excision is preferred. Consider local RT ± chemotherapy as an alternative for inadequate margins. Many patients have concomitant HSIL of the anal canal, so evaluate for anal canal involvement.
special_situations.evidence: NCCN category 2A recommendation. Adequate margin defined as 1 cm by ASCRS.

clinical_pearls: The superior border of the functional anal canal (anorectal ring) separates it from the rectum. Tumors that cannot be entirely seen when buttocks are pressed are anal canal cancers; those that can be visualized are perianal cancers.
Concurrent chemoradiation is the standard of care for locoregional anal carcinoma, avoiding the morbidity of a permanent colostomy associated with primary APR.

Mitomycin is a critical component of the concurrent chemoradiation regimen. The phase III Intergroup study showed a lower colostomy rate (9% vs. 22%) and higher 4-year DFS (73% vs. 51%) with mitomycin/5-FU vs. 5-FU alone.

The ACT-II trial showed no benefit of maintenance chemotherapy or replacement of mitomycin with cisplatin in the chemoRT regimen. Importantly, it also demonstrated that persistent disease may continue to regress up to 26 weeks from treatment start.
IMRT is preferred over 3D conformal RT to reduce acute toxicities (especially dermatologic and gastrointestinal) without compromising local control, as demonstrated in RTOG 0529.
The POD1UM-303/InterAACT2 trial established carboplatin/paclitaxel + retifanlimab as the new preferred first-line regimen for metastatic anal cancer, showing improved PFS over chemotherapy alone.

HPV vaccination is recommended for prevention of anal HPV infection and related lesions. The 9-valent vaccine (Gardasil 9) is now the standard, with demonstrated efficacy in preventing HPV-6/11/16/18-related disease.
For patients with persistent disease post-chemoradiation without progression, observation for up to 6 months is appropriate, as complete responses can still be achieved, potentially avoiding an APR.

guidelines_resources.notes: Includes the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which covers social determinants of health.

guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for information on DPYD testing and fluoropyrimidine-associated toxicity.

guidelines_resources.title: Consensus Guidelines for Anal Cancer Screening
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides risk stratification and recommended screening strategies for anal HSIL to prevent anal cancer.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 9th Edition
guidelines_resources.notes: Defines the TNM staging system used for anal carcinoma.

key_trials.full_name: European Organization for Research and Treatment of Cancer Trial 22861
key_trials.key_result: Chemoradiation superior: 18% higher locoregional control rate and 32% longer colostomy-free interval at 5 years.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No significant difference in OS at 5 years.
key_trials.practice_change: Established chemoradiation as superior to radiation alone for locally advanced anal cancer.

key_trials.full_name: UK Coordinating Committee on Cancer Research Anal Cancer Trial I
key_trials.key_result: Chemoradiation superior for local control (RR, 0.54; 95% CI, 0.42–0.69; P < .0001). 13-year follow-up showed OS benefit (HR, 0.67; 95% CI, 0.51–0.88; P = .004).
key_trials.practice_change: Confirmed chemoradiation as standard of care over radiation alone.

key_trials.key_result: 5-FU/mitomycin superior: 5-year DFS 67.8% vs. 57.8% (P = .006). 5-year OS also superior (78.3% vs. 70.7%; P = .026).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: 5-year colostomy-free survival trended better with mitomycin (71.9% vs. 65.0%; P = .05).
key_trials.practice_change: Established 5-FU/mitomycin as the standard concurrent chemotherapy regimen over 5-FU/cisplatin. Suggested induction chemotherapy is detrimental.

key_trials.intervention: Two factorial: 1) Cisplatin-based vs. Mitomycin-based chemoRT; 2) Maintenance chemo (5-FU/cisplatin) vs. no maintenance
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Complete response rate (chemoRT comparison); Progression-free survival (maintenance comparison)
key_trials.key_result: No difference between cisplatin and mitomycin arms. No benefit for maintenance chemotherapy.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No difference in colostomy rates. 72% of patients without complete response at 11 weeks achieved it by 26 weeks.
key_trials.practice_change: Cisplatin is an acceptable alternative to mitomycin. Maintenance chemotherapy is not recommended. Established that persistent disease can regress for up to 6 months.

key_trials.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology, Biology, Physics
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Grade ≥2 combined acute genitourinary and gastrointestinal adverse events
key_trials.key_result: Did not meet primary endpoint (77% vs. 77%). However, significant reductions in grade ≥2 hematologic (73% vs. 85%; P = .032), grade ≥3 GI (21% vs. 36%; P = .008), and grade ≥3 dermatologic (23% vs. 49%; P < .0001) toxicities.
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated reduced acute toxicity with IMRT, leading to its preferred status over 3D-CRT for anal cancer treatment.

key_trials.full_name: International Rare Cancers Initiative Multicenter Randomized Phase II Trial
key_trials.population: Advanced/metastatic anal squamous cell carcinoma, first-line
key_trials.key_result: Similar response rates (59% vs. 57%). Carboplatin/paclitaxel had lower grade ≥3 toxicity (71% vs. 76%) and fewer serious adverse events (36% vs. 62%; P = .016).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median PFS 8.1 vs. 5.7 months. Median OS 20 vs. 12.3 months (HR, 2.0; 95% CI, 1.15–3.47; P = .014).
key_trials.practice_change: Established carboplatin/paclitaxel as a less toxic and potentially more effective first-line regimen than cisplatin/5-FU for metastatic anal cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Phase 3 study of retifanlimab with carboplatin-paclitaxel in patients with inoperable locally recurrent or metastatic SCAC
key_trials.population: First-line metastatic/inoperable locally recurrent anal squamous cell carcinoma
key_trials.key_result: PFS improved: median 9.30 months (95% CI, 7.5–11.3) vs. 7.39 months (95% CI, 7.1–7.7); HR = 0.63; P = .0006.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS trended favorable: 29.2 months vs. 23.0 months.
key_trials.practice_change: Established carboplatin/paclitaxel + retifanlimab as the new preferred first-line regimen for metastatic anal cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab in patients with previously treated advanced anal squamous cell carcinoma
key_trials.population: Previously treated advanced anal squamous cell carcinoma
key_trials.key_result: ORR 11% (95% CI, 6–18). Higher in PD-L1-positive (15%) vs. PD-L1-negative (3%).
key_trials.practice_change: Supported use of pembrolizumab in second-line metastatic anal cancer, regardless of PD-L1 status.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab for previously treated unresectable metastatic anal cancer
key_trials.key_result: Response rate 24% (95% CI, 15–33). Two complete responses.
key_trials.practice_change: Provided early evidence for checkpoint inhibitor activity in anal cancer, leading to inclusion of nivolumab as a second-line option.

key_trials.population: People living with HIV with anal high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL)
key_trials.intervention: Topical or ablative treatment of HSIL
key_trials.comparator: Active monitoring with HRA every 6 months
key_trials.key_result: Treatment reduced progression to anal cancer by 57% (95% CI, 6–80; P = .03). 9 vs. 21 cancer cases in treatment vs. monitoring groups.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Progression rate from HSIL to cancer was 402/100,000 person-years with monitoring.
key_trials.practice_change: Provides high-level evidence supporting treatment of anal HSIL to prevent anal cancer in high-risk populations.
